subject: Astronomy & Space
title: Planetary Nebulae: Stellar Endings
description: A quick, practical guide to Planetary Nebulae: Stellar Endings.

hook.text: Planetary nebulae are shells of gas shed by dying Sun-like stars. They have nothing to do with planets—the name is misleading.

buildup.text: When a star like the Sun exhausts its fuel, it puffs out its outer layers, leaving a hot white dwarf at the center.

discovery.text: The Ring Nebula and Helix Nebula are famous examples. They glow in blues, greens, and reds from different ionized elements.

twist.text: William Herschel named them in 1785 because they looked like planets through his telescope. The name stuck despite being wrong.

climax.text: In about 5 billion years, the Sun will create its own planetary nebula. Earth will likely be engulfed long before that.

punchline.text: Not planets at all. Dying stars blowing beautiful bubbles before fading to white.

quiz.question: Why are planetary nebulae misnamed?
options: They looked like planet disks through early telescopes, but they're actually shells from dying stars
They were originally thought to form new planets from their gas
The first ones were discovered near planets in the solar system
